compiled_state.author: Joe Oakhart
compiled_state.description:
## Summary
Session logs "bleed" between sessions: a new agent session appends to the previous
session's `session_log` ticket instead of starting a fresh one, producing a single
enormous log that combines multiple unrelated sessions.

## Root cause (suspected)
The "current session log" is tracked by a **local, git-ignored pointer**,
`.rebar/current_session_log`. `append_session_log` / `log_session` / `session-log append`
create a log on first use and then append to whatever the pointer names. The pointer is
**never scoped to the actual session/agent** and is never auto-rotated at session
boundaries — so once written it persists across sessions, and every later session's
appends land on the same old log ticket until someone manually runs
`session-log start`. rebar has no session identifier of its own (see related epic
`crust-fetch-stump`), so it cannot detect that a new session has begun.

## Reproduction Steps
1. In session A: `rebar session-log append "work on thing A"` (creates a log, writes the pointer).
2. Finish session A without ever calling `session-log start`.
3. In an unrelated session B (same checkout): `rebar session-log append "work on thing B"`.
4. Inspect the log: `rebar session-logs --limit 5` / `rebar show <log-id>`.

- **Expected:** session B's entry is in its own new `session_log` (or at least clearly
  attributable to session B); logs stay per-session and reasonably sized.
- **Actual:** session B appends to session A's log via the stale
  `.rebar/current_session_log` pointer; the single log grows without bound and mixes
  entries from multiple unrelated sessions, making retrieval-by-keyword noisy and the
  bodies huge.

## Impact
- Session logs become extremely long and mix unrelated work, defeating their purpose as
  durable, per-session, keyword-retrievable records.
- Large combined bodies are exactly what the `session_log` type was designed to keep out
  of the hot paths; a giant shared log undermines that intent.

## Acceptance Criteria
- [ ] A new session does not silently append to a prior session's `session_log`; the
      first `append` in a new session writes to a fresh log (auto-rotation) or the pointer
      is scoped so cross-session bleed cannot happen.
- [ ] The rotation/scoping mechanism is documented (CLAUDE.md session-logs section +
      `docs/` as appropriate) and does not require agents to remember `session-log start`.
- [ ] Backward compatible: existing pointer/log behavior for a single continuous session
      is unchanged; absent any session identifier the behavior degrades safely.
- [ ] A regression test demonstrates that two distinct sessions produce distinct logs
      (or the same log only when genuinely the same session).
compiled_state.title: Session logs bleed between sessions — pointer never rotates, producing one giant multi-session log
